symbols: Two figures locked in combat — the struggle between the human and the divine, Jacob's persistence in seeking blessing
Autumn woodland setting — the liminal space of Peniel, between the old life and the new
Stream or path alongside the figures — the Jabbok river where the wrestling took place
description: Two figures in dark blue garments grapple with each other in a woodland clearing. Tall slender tree trunks rise around them, their autumn foliage rendered in bold oranges and golds. A winding stream or path runs alongside the figures through a green landscape. The palette is rich with deep blues, vivid oranges, and emerald greens, rendered in flat decorative shapes characteristic of post-impressionist design.
theological_description: Jacob's wrestling at the Jabbok (GEN.32.24-32) is one of the Old Testament's most mysterious and profound episodes. On the night before his dreaded reunion with Esau, Jacob is left alone and wrestles with a 'man' until daybreak. When the being cannot prevail, he touches Jacob's hip and dislocates it, then bestows the new name Israel — 'one who strives with God.' Jacob names the place Peniel, 'for I have seen God face to face, and yet my life has been delivered' (GEN.32.30). The Church Fathers read this encounter christologically: Origen and others saw the mysterious wrestler as the pre-incarnate Logos. The episode also serves as the paradigm of prayer — persistent, costly, and transformative. Jacob will not release his adversary without a blessing, embodying the tenacity Jesus commended in the parable of the persistent widow (LUK.18.1-8). The wound Jacob receives — a permanent limp — signifies that encounter with God always leaves us changed.

scripture_refs.reason: Jacob wrestling with the angel at the Jabbok, the specific event depicted
Jacob receives the name Israel: 'You have striven with God and with men, and have prevailed'
Hosea recalls Jacob wrestling with the angel and weeping for blessing, interpreting the encounter
Persistent prayer rewarded — Jacob's refusal to let go without blessing parallels the persistent widow
Jacob names the place Peniel: 'I have seen God face to face and my life is preserved'
